title: The ethics of eating meat vs. plants

chunks.title: First Experience with Meat
chunks.summary: The speaker recounts their initial experiences with eating meat, noting the emotional impact it had on them. They describe feeling conflicted due to societal beliefs they internalized about meat consumption being harmful.
chunks.transcript: do you remember the first time you ate meat um I yeah yeah I first started incorporating eggs and then a little bit of fish and then I had the liver before I had the steak okay that's the way to do it was there like maybe there wasn't but was there like a euphoric moment where you and when you ate the liver or when you ate the eggs or these nutrient-rich Foods came back from your diet did you feel it what is so bizarre to me is the emotional response that happened immediately when I was eating meat again like I was cutting up this steak and bawling my eyes out because intuitively I knew that it was like the right step to improving my health like incorporating meat again but like I had just been so programmed to believe um that meat was like hurting you know the planet and really bad for like our ecosystems and the animals and like it was this emotional um tie that I had like I felt like it was a bad person for eating this meat even though it was like the best thing for me at the time so yeah the first time I had meat and the times afterwards like I just had to work through the guilt that had been programmed into me

chunks.title: Programming and Beliefs about Food
chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the concept of societal programming and unexamined beliefs related to food. They discuss how people often accept beliefs about nutrition and health without critically questioning their origins.
chunks.transcript: and I was like crying all those vegan documentaries just like getting in your head yeah and even though I'd done the research and known that you know there's vegan documentaries are misleading I still had that like programming wired into me um it's so hard to unprogram ourselves isn't it like it's crazy to think about all the things that we believe that we don't know why we believe them I mean I talk to people all the time and different things come up you know I'll tell people like an interesting one is I'll tell people I don't use toothpaste and they're they their eyes get wide like this they're like dinner so we're in suitcase I'm thinking why do you use two things and they they just don't even understand what's in your toothpaste yeah it can sit in yeah it's so interesting to talk to people and to just say to them like I don't use toothpaste and to see what happens to me and then to ask them a question like why do you use Too Faced isn't that it's usually a question back to me like isn't that necessary to clean your teeth and anything you don't even know you know and you're using it and then like you said what's in your toothpaste or what's in your deodorant or what's in your skin moisturizer or what's in your I mean what's in your anything right where is your food coming from yes all the constant questioning which yeah I don't know we just accept this yeah yeah and people you know I think people will have the same reaction if I say I eat a lot of red meat and they don't eat red meat or they don't eat a lot of red isn't red meat bad for you why do you believe that red meat is bad for you and people I just think that there's a lot of us who a lot of us believe things and we don't know why we believe them and I'm sure that I have beliefs that are unexamined and sort of untested but it's interesting to think as a human how many of the things that we use to navigate our life every day our decisions based

chunks.title: Cultural Influences on Dietary Choices
chunks.summary: The discussion explores how cultural narratives surrounding food impact personal choices, especially regarding cholesterol and egg consumption. The speaker reflects on how long-held beliefs can be difficult to alter, particularly among older generations.
chunks.transcript: on programming that isn't really ours totally I love because I I recently went to visit my parents and I'm spending some time with them and I was eating like eggs every day and I knew from my dad you know isn't cholesterol bad for you and I know that he's just like referencing an ad about eggs that happens on TV like 20 years ago yeah it's like cholesterol is bad narrative is being pushed so much it's still pushed now but like you know they used to have ads on TV about it about how bad it was yeah yeah did you ask your dad why do you believe that or what do you think do you think that that's the programming that he has does he know I just told him that you know the the narrative changes and you know this updated information now and um yeah it's hard it's hard when somebody's um believed something for so long but my parents are both 72. it's hard to help them make changes I've almost stopped at this point because they're so

chunks.title: Ethical Considerations in Meat Consumption
chunks.summary: The focus turns to the ethical implications of eating animal products versus prioritizing personal health. The speaker explains their journey in sourcing ethical meat and how different farming practices affect animal welfare.

chunks.title: The Cycle of Life and Death
chunks.summary: The speaker reflects on the natural cycle of life and death, explaining that to sustain life, other lives must be sacrificed. They question the moral hierarchy placed on different living beings in the context of food consumption.
chunks.transcript: meat products that I could yeah it's interesting there's this great book that I read a number of years ago called the tracker by Tom Brown and he's this this man who grew up in the tactics hunting tactics and in engagement with the land from this Native American sort of at like the nadir of like you know like like the end of like the Native American sort of like history right because it was probably the end of that Native Americans family and like their their cultures are being you know being lost in this time I think he was in the 70s so it's a couple it was like a couple chunks.title: Critique of Vegan and Vegetarian Ethics
chunks.summary: The critique of vegan and vegetarian arguments is presented, suggesting that these views often lack a mature philosophical understanding of life and death in the ecosystem. The conversation emphasizes a disconnect with food sources and the responsibilities of food production.
chunks.transcript: cycle of life and death but I've always felt like these ethical vegan arguments or vegetarian arguments mostly the vegan arguments are sort of this like false this false prioritization of like neurological Evolution just because a cow has a brain and walks on four legs and moves who's to say that the life force and that cow is different than the kale plant that you're killing or what about all of the microorganisms in the field that you're disrupting in the worms like the cow's life is more valuable than an a mouse's life and it's like well I mean I think we would as humans like a reasonably empathetic human we feel bad about just maliciously killing a mouse or a bowl or a snake why do we prioritize a bigger animal it's kind of just this fake human construct right and yeah I think that like this idea of by kills and the fact that when you take a field or a forest that you have to chop before us you're killing all the trees so all of that life force is going back to the life force well or wherever it goes and then all of the animals that depend on those trees are gone then you have to cut the trees up and you have to till the soil and then you have to kill everything that's in the soil that you're tilling and then all the rabbits all the Wolves all the mice all the Badgers and you know snakes and all these lives are disruptive all the rabbits but we're saying no that's not bad but what is bad is killing a cow that grazes on the grass and lives a life and will die you know as part of their life cycle anyway it's it's interesting I think that a lot of the vegan and vegetarian ethos and ethics are well intentioned they're just not they don't seem philosophically mature

chunks.title: Connection to Nature and Dietary Practices
chunks.summary: The discussion concludes with a meditation on the importance of connecting with nature in understanding dietary practices. Both speakers emphasize the necessity of recognizing our roots and the impact of modern technology on our relationship with food.
